frames.name: Justice and accountability frame
frames.description: Emphasizes the successful prosecution and conviction as a victory for justice after years of struggle
frames.emphasis: The eight-year fight for justice, family satisfaction with verdicts, and the judicial system's vindication

frames.name: Political corruption and organized crime frame
frames.description: Focuses on the deep ties between politicians and militia groups, presenting the case as exposing systemic corruption
frames.emphasis: The brothers' militia connections, land development schemes, and broader political-criminal networks

frames.name: Social justice and identity frame
frames.description: Highlights Franco as a gay Black woman activist fighting for marginalized communities against powerful interests
frames.emphasis: Franco's identity, her advocacy for favela residents, and the intersection of gender and racial violence

frames.name: Procedural legal frame
frames.description: Presents the story primarily as a criminal case with focus on convictions, sentences, and legal proceedings
frames.emphasis: Specific sentences, charges, legal procedures, and the mechanics of the prosecution

outlierPerspectives: The Guardian's emphasis on the 'tortuous series of twists' including evidence destruction and investigator changes
Justice Cármen Lúcia's emotional commentary about 'spiritual and psychological harm' and the inadequacy of human justice
Monica Benicio's (Franco's widow) warning that the 'criminal ecosystem that killed Marielle continues to operate'
missingContext: The specific housing development projects that motivated the murder
The broader scope and power of Rio's militia groups beyond this case
The political and social impact of Franco's murder on Brazilian politics and activism
Details about how the investigation was initially compromised and by whom
The current state of militia control in Rio de Janeiro neighborhoods
